title: Lab 1 · Statistics in Excel
summary: The descriptive half of the first lab. You summarise a data sample with the standard estimators (AVERAGE, MEDIAN, MODE, VAR.S, STDEV.S, QUARTILE), read probabilities off the **Normal** distribution with NORM.DIST / NORM.INV, and build a **confidence interval for the mean** with the **Student t**. Every result is obtained by typing a real Excel formula and pressing *Check*.
objectives: Compute the full set of descriptive statistics of a sample, choosing sample (n−1) over population estimators.
Read 'less than', 'greater than' and interval probabilities from NORM.DIST with the cumulative flag set to TRUE.
Standardise a value to a z-score and use the standard normal NORM.S.DIST / NORM.S.INV.
Invert the normal with NORM.INV to find the value not exceeded with a given probability.
Build a confidence interval for the mean with the Student t: half-width t·s/√n using T.INV.2T.
Apply the same machinery to a type-II error (β) and a bilateral mean test.

blocks.text: Function primer — descriptive statistics

Before any inference, you summarise the sample. Each estimator is one Excel call over the data range. Learn the **sample vs population** distinction: in metrology your data are almost always a *sample* of a larger production, so you use the $n-1$ forms.
Arithmetic mean $\bar{x}=\frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$ — the centre of mass of the data.
Middle value of the sorted data (mean of the two central values if $n$ is even). Robust to outliers; when median ≈ mean the sample is roughly symmetric.
Most frequently occurring value. MODE.SNGL is the modern name; both return the same single mode.

**Sample** variance $s^2=\frac{1}{n-1}\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2$ and its square root $s=\sqrt{s^2}$. The $n-1$ divisor (Bessel's correction) is what distinguishes these from the population forms VAR.P / STDEV.P (which divide by $n$). Use the `.S` forms for a sample.
Smallest value, largest value, and the number of numeric cells $n$. $\text{MAX}-\text{MIN}$ is the range; COUNT gives the sample size used everywhere downstream.
Quartile selected by the integer $q$: 0 = minimum, 1 = $Q_1$ (25th pct), 2 = median, 3 = $Q_3$ (75th pct), 4 = maximum. The interquartile range $Q_3-Q_1$ is a robust spread measure.

blocks.title: Sample or population?
blocks.text: VAR.S / STDEV.S divide by $n-1$; VAR.P / STDEV.P divide by $n$. The exam data are a **sample**, so the `.S` forms are correct. Picking `.P` is a silent error — the number looks plausible but is too small.

blocks.title: Exercise 1 — descriptive statistics of a sample

blocks.text: Function primer — the Normal distribution

The normal (Gaussian) distribution models continuous measurements. Excel gives you a **direct** function (value → probability) and an **inverse** function (probability → value), in both a general and a standardised form.

For a normal with mean $m$ and standard deviation $s$:
- **cumulative = 1 / TRUE** → the CDF $P(X\le x)=F(x)$, the area to the **left** of $x$ (the S-shaped curve).
- **cumulative = 0 / FALSE** → the density $f(x)$, the height of the bell curve at $x$.
For probabilities you almost always want the cumulative form (1).
The **standard** normal ($m=0,\,s=1$). It needs only the standardised value $z=\dfrac{x-m}{s}$ and the cumulative flag. $\text{NORM.S.DIST}(z;1)=\text{NORM.DIST}(x;m;s;1)$ — the two are equivalent.

The **inverse** CDF: given a left-cumulative probability $P$ they return the value $x$ (resp. the $z$) with $P(X\le x)=P$. There is no cumulative argument — the inverse always works on the cumulative probability. Link: $x=m+z\,s$ where $z=\text{NORM.S.INV}(P)$.

blocks.caption: An interval probability is the difference of two CDF values — subtract, never add.

blocks.title: The three readings of a normal probability
blocks.text:
- 'Less than $x$': $F(x)=\text{NORM.DIST}(x;m;s;1)$.
- 'Greater than $x$': $1-F(x)$ (Excel only gives the left tail).
- 'Between $a$ and $b$': $F(b)-F(a)$.
For inverse problems: a **lower** boundary at risk $\alpha$ is $\text{NORM.INV}(\alpha;m;s)$; an **upper** boundary at risk $\alpha$ is $\text{NORM.INV}(1-\alpha;m;s)$.

blocks.title: Exercise 2 — probability in an interval (NORM.DIST)
Exercise 3 — standardisation and the standard normal
Exercise 4 — inverse normal (NORM.INV / NORM.S.INV)
Inverse boundary: pass the LEFT-cumulative probability
blocks.text: NORM.INV always takes the cumulative probability *to the left* of the value it returns. For an **upper** limit leaving a right-tail risk $\alpha_s$, feed $1-\alpha_s$, not $\alpha_s$. Feeding the tail risk directly is the single most common exam slip on inverse problems.
Function primer — the Student t and confidence intervals

When the population standard deviation $\sigma$ is **unknown** and estimated from the sample, the sample mean follows a **Student t** distribution with $n-1$ degrees of freedom, not a normal. The t curve is bell-shaped but has heavier tails; as $n$ grows it approaches the normal.
CDF of the t distribution with $\nu$ degrees of freedom. With **cumulative = 1 / TRUE** it returns the left-tail $P(T\le t)$; with **0 / FALSE** the density. Right tail = $1-\text{T.DIST}(t;\nu;1)$.

blocks.caption: Confidence interval for the mean with σ unknown: half-width = critical t × standard error.

blocks.title: Why /√n?
blocks.text: The data have spread $s$, but the **sample mean** is far less variable: its standard deviation is the standard error $s/\sqrt{n}$. Averaging $n$ readings shrinks the uncertainty by $\sqrt{n}$ — that is the factor inside every confidence interval and mean test.

blocks.title: Exercise 5 — confidence interval for the mean (Student t)

blocks.text: Putting it together: errors and tests

The same two functions (NORM.INV for limits, NORM.DIST for probabilities) drive the real exam questions: the **risk of error of the second kind β**, a **bilateral mean test**, and an **equal-risk separation limit** between two distributions.

blocks.title: Exercise 6 — risk of error of the second kind β
Exercise 7 — bilateral test on a repaired machine
Exercise 8 — equal-risk separation limit
Variance vs standard deviation
blocks.text: NORM.DIST and NORM.INV take the **standard deviation** $s$, never the variance. When the data give you $\sigma^2$ (as in the separation and β problems), pass $\text{SQRT}(\sigma^2)$. Plugging the variance straight in is a classic, hard-to-spot error.
Distributions for tests (reference)

Two more distributions complete the toolkit: the **Student t** for inference on the mean (above) and the **Pearson χ²** / **Fisher F** for variance and variance-ratio tests. Explore their shapes and tails below, then practise the t exercise.

blocks.title: Exercise 9 — Student t tails and inverse
Exercise 10 — merged supplies (hypergeometric acceptance)

blocks.text: More statistics exercises

The rest of the lab works through every remaining exercise from the source sheets. **Acceptance sampling** is the recurring theme: you decide whether to accept a batch from a sample, choosing between the **binomial** (large batch), the **hypergeometric** (finite batch, no replacement) and the **Poisson** (rare-event) models. Then come the remaining **risk** and **distribution-function** exercises. Each is one interactive grid — type the formula, press *Check*.

blocks.title: Which distribution for acceptance sampling?
blocks.text:
- **Binomial** $\text{BINOM.DIST}(k;n;p;1)$ — large batch (or sample $\ll$ batch); draws treated as independent.
- **Hypergeometric** $\text{HYPGEOM.DIST}(k;n;C;M;1)$ — finite batch of $M$ with $C$ defectives, drawn **without replacement**; exact.
- **Poisson** $\text{POISSON.DIST}(k;\lambda;1)$ with $\lambda=np$ — approximation for large $n$, small $p$.
'Accept if no more than $k$' is always the **cumulative** form (last flag = 1).

Exercise 19 — two-component mechanism: failure risk and inverse design
Exercise 20 — Poisson approximation of an acceptance plan
Exercise 21 — separation limit with a rectangular error
Pearson χ² and Fisher F
blocks.text: These two distributions read exactly like the Normal and Student t: cumulative = 1 gives the left tail, the right tail is $1-F$, an interval is $F(b)-F(a)$, and the inverse maps a left-cumulative probability back to a value. Both are **right-skewed** (positive support), so their lower and upper boundaries are not symmetric. F takes **two** degrees of freedom — numerator first, then denominator.

blocks.title: Exercise 22 — Pearson χ² tails and inverse
Exercise 23 — Fisher F tails and inverse

practice.prompt: You measured a **sample** of 30 parts and want the typical spread of the readings. Which function should you use?
practice.explanation: The 30 parts are a **sample** of a larger production, so the unbiased estimator divides by $n-1$: that is STDEV.S. A is wrong because STDEV.P (divide by $n$) is for a full population and underestimates the spread; C returns a variance (squared units) and also uses the population divisor; D returns the mean, not the spread.
practice.theory: Use the `.S` forms (VAR.S / STDEV.S, $n-1$) for samples and the `.P` forms ($n$) only when the data are the entire population.

practice.prompt: For $X\sim N(85,1.2)$ you want $P(X>86.2)$. Which formula is correct?
practice.explanation: Excel's cumulative NORM.DIST returns the **left** tail $P(X\le x)$, so the right tail is the complement $1-F(x)$ — option C. A gives $P(X\le86.2)$, the wrong side; B with cumulative = 0 returns the **density** (a curve height, not a probability); D is the inverse function and expects a probability as its first argument, not a value.
practice.theory: Greater-than probabilities are $1-\text{NORM.DIST}(x;m;s;1)$ because Excel only provides the left-tail CDF.

practice.prompt: What does the **last argument** of NORM.DIST(x; m; s; cumulative) control?

options.content: Whether the result is a percentage or a fraction
TRUE/1 → cumulative CDF $P(X\le x)$; FALSE/0 → density $f(x)$
The number of degrees of freedom
Whether to use the left or the right tail

practice.explanation: The cumulative flag switches between the two outputs: 1/TRUE gives the cumulative distribution $P(X\le x)$ (the S-curve), 0/FALSE gives the probability **density** $f(x)$ (the bell height). A is invented — Excel never rescales to a percentage. C confuses it with the t / χ² distributions, which take degrees of freedom as a separate argument. D is wrong: the function always returns the left side; you take the complement yourself for the right tail.
practice.theory: Across NORM.DIST, T.DIST, CHISQ.DIST and F.DIST the final logical argument means the same thing: 1 = cumulative CDF, 0 = density.

practice.prompt: You need the value $x$ **not exceeded with probability 0.95** for $X\sim N(85,1.2)$. Which formula gives it?
practice.explanation: 'Not exceeded with probability 0.95' means $P(X\le x)=0.95$, i.e. the 95th percentile — exactly $\text{NORM.INV}(0.95;m;s)$, option D. A subtracts a value (not a probability) from 1, which is meaningless. B feeds a probability into the *direct* function as if it were a value of $x$. C returns the 5th percentile (the value exceeded with probability 0.95), the opposite boundary.
practice.theory: NORM.INV(P; m; s) maps a left-cumulative probability to a value; the 95th percentile is NORM.INV(0.95; m; s).

practice.prompt: Building a 95 % confidence interval for the mean from $n=10$ readings with $\sigma$ unknown, which critical value and half-width are correct?
practice.explanation: With $\sigma$ unknown you use the Student t with $\nu=n-1=9$ degrees of freedom, and the two-tailed critical value is T.INV.2T(0.05; 9); the half-width is $t\cdot s/\sqrt{n}$ — option A. B uses the normal $z$, valid only when $\sigma$ is known. C uses $\nu=10$ (should be $n-1=9$) and omits the $/\sqrt{n}$ standard-error factor. D uses T.INV(0.05; 9), which returns the **negative** one-tailed quantile leaving 5 % in the left tail, not the two-tailed critical value (that would be T.INV(0.975; 9)).
practice.theory: CI for the mean, σ unknown: $\bar{x}\mp t_{1-\alpha/2,n-1}\,s/\sqrt{n}$ with $t_{1-\alpha/2,n-1}=\text{T.INV.2T}(\alpha;n-1)$.
